items.title: Spellbreaker Low Intensity Power 30k/35k/37k
items.explanation:
This is a beginner/low intensity warrior build for fractal/raid/strike content.

0:00 1 Button Rotation
2:36 1 Button Explanation
3:17 5 Buttons With Signets Rotation
5:34 5 Buttons With Signets Explanation
7:33 5 Buttons With Kick Rotation
9:41 5 Buttons With Kick Explanation
11:06 Overall Build Explanation
items.cleaned_transcript:
For the one-button breakdown, all you want to do is spam your F1, and that's pretty much it. Use To the Limit!, then spam F1 and autoattack. My only recommendation, if this is the build you're going to play and you're doing nothing but autoattacking and pressing F1, is to take dagger offhand to min-max Sun and Moon Style. That way you will heal for a percentage of your outgoing strike damage. Typically, I would recommend taking some form of CC, like a mace, or swapping one of your signets to some CC skills so you are able to CC. Skip ahead if you are looking for the build breakdown.

For the five-button signet breakdown, all you want to do is spam Dolyak Signet and Signet of Stamina. Your rotation is effectively: your opener is To the Limit! into your F1, into your skill four and skill five, then skill four on mace whenever it resets. Dolyak Signet and Signet of Stamina have no cast time, so you can use them whenever and they won't interrupt anything. For example, I can use them during my dagger two and it doesn't interrupt anything. Basically, you can use them as soon as they're up. You don't really have to think about it too much. It's pretty brain-off. Cast those signets.

For the last build I had for this build, it was dagger/axe. Dagger/axe is still completely fine. It's still playable. It's a tiny bit less damage on average, but dagger/axe is less spammy to play. You get a lot more sustain from Dual Wielding, so it has its advantages, but it also has disadvantages. Mace will do a little bit more damage. It has a little bit more burst damage as well because you can crank out 4-5-4 really quickly and do a lot of damage, and it has built-in CC. It is completely your preference if you want to play dagger/axe or dagger/mace. I'm going to switch the default to dagger/mace because I think it's a tiny bit stronger as an option. If you're going to be playing these builds in raids or fractals with a healer, you probably won't need that much sustain anyway, considering that you already have Dual Wielding. Dagger/axe and dagger/mace are both fairly equivalent. Skip ahead if you're looking for the build breakdown.

For five buttons with Kick, more or less what you want to do is time your Kick and Signet of Fury with any time your F1 is about to come up. Your opener is Signet of Fury into Kick, into F1, into 4-5-4. More or less, just follow the rule of any time your F1 is about to come up, you time your Kick, use your Signet of Fury, and then use your F1 into whatever is up.

The only thing with this build that makes it a little bit more active is that you want to save your mace four a little bit to time it with your Kick, Peak Performance, your Signet of Fury ferocity bonus, and, since this build runs Relic of the Claw, the Relic of the Claw buff. Basically, there are going to be times when your skill four is up, but you're going to wait until your next F1 is up. That way you can time your Kick into your Signet of Fury, into your F1, into 4-5-4. Any time you hit mace five, it will reset skill four, so not using skill four early is fine and is not a DPS loss.

For the build breakdown, dagger two is a leap, so you can use it to get around and get the extra mobility. Dagger three is a CC, so use it if you see a breakbar. It's actually also a tiny bit of a DPS increase for every build, but it's more of a DPS increase for the Kick build because you run Claw. Any time you use dagger three, you'll actually proc Claw. If you're looking to add a sixth button without weapon swapping, then Disrupting Stab is going to be your next best bet.

Skill four and skill five on mace are free to ignore if you don't care. If you're running axe, which again, in my opinion is still fine to run and is a decent option, the main way it works is that your skill five will actually reset your skill four, and skill five is also a CC. Skill four, then skill five resets your skill four, so you can crank out another skill four for a lot of burst damage.

For your traits, the Strength line is fairly standard. You take Peak Performance, which basically means any time you use a physical skill, you're going to do 10% more damage. This is not relevant outside of the Kick build. This build takes Signet Mastery. I don't abuse it in any of the rotations or benches that I show you today, but more or less what you can do before the fight starts is use all of your signets. You're going to get a 60-second Signet of Ferocity buff, which is basically 100 free ferocity per stack. Right now I have 400 free ferocity. You can basically sit there and use up all of your signets, the ones you don't really need throughout the fight, and get about 500 free ferocity at the start of the fight to crank out way more damage than you normally would. It just depends. If you're sitting there doing nothing before the fight, then you can use these to crank out a little extra DPS at the start.

This build also takes Unsuspecting Foe. What this means is that you are not actually crit-capped against enemies without a defiance bar. I would think twice about running it in open world, where you're just fighting random mobs, because basically you might see that you're not critting on open-world stuff. You should be able to crit on anything that is a boss with a breakbar. It doesn't matter if they currently have the breakbar active or not. As long as they have a breakbar that is either active, inactive, or grayed out, Unsuspecting Foe will kick in. This is how you crit cap. This is why the build, if you check your gear stats, does not reach 100% crit. It relies on Unsuspecting Foe to crit cap.

Dual Wielding gives a lot of extra healing. Pretty much all of the low-intensity warrior builds take Dual Wielding because it is good, and you want to buff your autoattacks because that's 90% of what you do in these builds anyway. The Spellbreaker line does not really have much to add. Just damage, damage, damage, healing, damage.

For your utilities, your healing skill is going to be To the Limit! if you're not running the Kick build. Otherwise, you are free to run whatever you want, really. Warrior has a lot of really selfish stuff. Mending is a good condi cleanse skill. Defiant Stance is a good invulnerability skill. I would probably recommend that you stick to Healing Signet and To the Limit!. Warrior already has high base armor, high base HP, and you already get so much sustain from Dual Wielding, so you probably don't need anything fancy for your healing, but those options are available if you want them.

Warrior has a few useful group utility skills. Banner of Defense is group aegis. Banner of Tactics is group stability. Shake It Off! is a group condi cleanse and a group stun break. Shake It Off! is probably overall the most useful, followed by the banners, if you're going to take something that is more utility-oriented for your group. In general, warrior is pretty selfish.

The bottom line here is all CC skills. For example, if you're lacking CC, feel free to swap in one of your utilities for one of these skills. Signet of Might you probably just want to leave untouched, but if you are really lacking CC and you really need to carry the CC, swapping out Signet of Might for a CC skill is going to be a DPS loss for you, but it is what it is.

For your elite, Signet of Rage can be taken as is. You can just use it any time there is downtime in the fight, and you get that ferocity buff. I would actually recommend trying Battle Standard because it is a group revive on a 90-second cooldown. I think this is really strong. It is really strong that you can run this as a DPS player and basically save the group in a really bad scenario. Think about Battle Standard or Signet of Rage.

Rampage, in general, I don't really see people running anymore, but it's a transformation skill that will give you a whole bunch of CC skills that you can use if you're really struggling for CC. In general, I would probably not use it, or use it just as a last resort, and stick to Signet of Rage or Battle Standard as you see fit. Thank you for watching, and I hope you enjoy the builds. I hope you see success with the builds. Thank you.

items.title: Paragon Low Intensity Condi 31k/38k
items.explanation:
This is a beginner/low intensity warrior build for fractal/raid/strike content.

0:00 1 Button Rotation
2:28 1 Button Explanation
3:50 4 Buttons Rotation
5:56 4 Buttons Explanation
7:06 Overall Build Explanation
items.cleaned_transcript:
For the one-button rundown, all you want to do is use "To the Limit!" to gain a whole bunch of adrenaline, use your F2 to gain two stacks of Berserker's Power, and use your F1 to gain the last two stacks and start the rotation. All you do is spam F1. That's it.

For the one-button Paragon, technically the gear I have in the build is not Snow Crows optimal. The gear is Viper's. The build itself relies mostly on bleeding, not purely bleeding because you have Relic of the Fractal, but basically you will be able to squeeze out around another thousand damage maybe from running full Grieving instead of full Viper's. Grieving does not give expertise, so with Grieving, instead of Trapper rune you would run Krait rune to get more bleeding duration. My advice is don't bother. It's not really worth optimizing the one-button build, guys. But if you really want to, take a look at Grieving plus Krait rune to maximize your bleed duration, and you will be able to squeeze a little bit more damage out of it.

For the four-button rundown, your opener is Signet of Fury into your F2 with Chant of Action, into your F1. Again, every time you use an F skill you get stacks of Berserker's Power. This will give you more damage starting out, and then you want to use your skill three and skill four on sword, and that's it. Your priority if all three skills are up is F1 over skill three over skill four.

This build spams Signet of Stamina. Again, this signet is the lowest-cooldown signet. You can also technically take Dolyak Signet if you want, but your damage will be a tiny bit lower because it has a higher cooldown. All you want to do is spam the signet. This will give you 100 free ferocity for 60 seconds every time you use it. In the opener, I precast all of my signets for that reason, so I can get a whole bunch of stacks of ferocity before I start the fight, and then I start the fight using F2, F1, etc.

For the overall build rundown, I'll go through your skills, traits, etc. Sword two is a big leap, really good for just getting around. Skill three and skill four are just big damage. Be a little bit careful with skill four, because the way it works is that you throw your sword at the enemy, the sword sticks in there, and then it applies torment. Make sure you don't yank it out early with Rip. Skill five is a block, so you can use skill five as a substitute for dodging an attack, and this is a DPS increase if you can hit it. It is combat dependent.

For your traits, there is not that much to note. I already talked to you about Signet Mastery. The way it works is that every time you cast a signet, you gain stacks of ferocity. If you're sitting there doing nothing before a boss is about to start, you can use your signets to basically get five stacks, or 500 ferocity, for free before the fight even starts, and that will increase your damage. Dual Wielding will heal you. Every time you hit an opponent with an autoattack or anything, this will heal you every time you strike them.

Again, you're playing Paragon. The big callout here is that there are two big callouts with Paragon in general: motivation and chants. Depending on your active refrain or chant, you will gain a different bonus from Strength in Numbers. What you want to do is make sure that you are sitting on Chant of Action. Be careful that you don't randomly use another one of your chants and then not switch back to Chant of Action, because otherwise you will be losing out on a whole bunch of extra damage.

The second thing with Paragon is that you want to maintain seven-plus stacks of motivation, because if you look at this trait, it gives you more damage the more motivation you have. The easy way you can tell that you have enough motivation is by looking at yourself and seeing if you have wings. For example, when I start the fight, you are going to see that I have golden wings because I have seven-plus stacks of motivation. Then you are going to see that I have seven stacks, and I am about to lose my golden wings. Congratulations, you suck at Paragon if you lose your gold wings.

Basically, that's the visual tell. You want to make sure that you don't drop your wings. If you don't want to look down at the bottom and ask, "Do I have seven-plus motivation?" that is the visual tell. If you lose your wings, you want to try to use your F skills or cycle through your F skills to gain back your wings.

For utilities, healing-wise, "We Shall Return!" gives a little bit of condition cleanse. I would probably run "Shake It Off!" over this, but I probably wouldn't mess with your healing skill too much. For your elite, you can run Battle Standard if you want. This is pretty good because, as a DPS player with this build, it is pretty good to have an ability to res five people on a 90-second cooldown, and you don't really lose out on that much if you don't run Signet of Rage. You can also run Rampage if you need CC, because starting out, this build kind of has no CC.

I would probably generally recommend that you swap out your Signet of Stamina for a CC skill for fights that do have CC, because Signet of Stamina is a very minor DPS buff, and you having CC is way more important. You can look at this bottom row and choose a CC skill to run. For example, you can run Bull's Charge.

If you're looking for a little bit more utility out of this build for your group, Banner of Defense gives barrier and aegis, Banner of Tactics gives stability, and "Shake It Off!" gives group condition cleanse and stun break. You can consider running any of these bottom skills for CC. There is also "On Your Knees!", which again is just another CC skill and pretty good to run.

If you want to, you can also run Dolyak Signet if you're looking for more selfish stability over Signet of Stamina. If you're going to be running the signet, this one is a little bit more selfish, but will give you more stability, and the trade-off for damage is honestly probably not going to be noticeable.

You can also play this build as an alacrity giver. If you look at your last trait in Paragon, you are going to see Feverish Pulse. If you slot in Feverish Pulse instead of Brisk Pacing, you will lose out on a whole bunch of damage, but you will now be able to give alacrity. The way this works is that every time you use one of your chants, you will pulse out about seven seconds of alacrity.

As an example, I will use my Signet of Fury, and then I will use my F2. You are going to see that I gave a little bit of alacrity. This alacrity is going to go away, and then I am going to recast my F2, and then I am going to give more alacrity. The way you want to use this, if you do decide to play this, is to cycle through your F skills, or chants, whatever you want to call them. Then you give a whole bunch of alacrity.

Basically, when you start the fight, you just cycle through F3, F2, F1 when you have your full adrenaline, and then passively use the chants as you see fit. Again, try to stick to F2 because it gives you a little bit more damage, and that is Chant of Action. That is that first chant. Try to stick on this one, and try to just quickly cycle through the other ones.

items.title: Berserker Low Intensity Condi 32k/36k
items.explanation:
This is a beginner/low intensity warrior build for fractal/raid/strike content.

0:00 3 Buttons Rotation
2:24 3 Buttons Explanation
3:13 5 Buttons Rotation
5:23 5 Buttons Explanation
6:37 Overall Build Explanation
items.cleaned_transcript:
To explain the three buttons, your opener is Head Butt. Whenever you hit it, you will gain full adrenaline, but you are going to stun yourself. You want to use Outrage to break that stun and start your combo. Basically, your combo is Berserk, F1, heal, F1, and then whenever Outrage is up, you just want to press it. It will increase the duration of your Berserk, and it will increase the duration of Berserk even more if you are close to an opponent. Spam your Outrage, spam your F1s, and whenever your heal skill is up, use your heal skill to put out another F1.

To explain the five buttons, your opener is Head Butt. If you hit this, it will give you full adrenaline, and then you can use Outrage to break that stun from Head Butt because this does give you a self-stun of one second. Use Outrage to break that stun. Then your combo is F1, heal, F1, three and four on sword, more or less. The priority for this rotation is that you want to use your F1 whenever it is up. You want to use your heal skill into F1 to reset it whenever it is up. Then your skill three and skill four are last in priority. So F1, heal into F1 if available, then skill three and skill four on sword, and use Outrage off cooldown.

Make sure you do not yank out your sword too early. If you look at skill four, the way it works is that you throw your sword at your opponent, and then you have the ability to rip it out of them. Make sure you do not do that. Just leave it in the opponent and let those torment stacks add up.

To explain the build overall, sword 2 is a big leap and movement ability. Three and four are big damage. You want to make sure to leave skill four inside the opponent. As I have already talked about, skill five is a DPS increase if you hit it. Skill five is a block, and if you end up blocking something, you basically end up doing a big bleeding hit. So it is a DPS increase if you can hit it.

For warrior traits, nothing is super fancy. You will take Dual Wielding, as pretty much all of these better low-intensity warrior builds do. This will heal you, so it is big healing for you from Dual Wielding. I found that Signet Mastery and Wounding Precision for this build were similar in terms of performance, so feel free to take whatever you want. One is more condi-favored, and one is more power-favored. I took the condi-favored one because the damage was very similar.

Typically, Berserker requires you to use your rage skills to maintain Berserk. You do not take Last Blaze with this build because it is low intensity. You take these signets, and you only spam one rage skill, so you actually get away with maintaining Berserk with Smash Brawler. Unfortunately, the build is not quite perfect, so you will likely run out of Berserk here and there. The hope is that you have to use Head Butt once in a while, or the phases make it so Berserk does not really matter. If you are in an extended fight, I recommend you either slot in another rage skill and use it, or use your Head Butt once in a while to increase the duration of your Berserk. This build can run out of Berserk pretty easily, especially if you end up in a phased part where you cannot hit your F1s or your Outrage.

This build can play quickness. The way it works is that, for your last trait in Berserker, you can slot in Heat the Soul, and every time you hit an F1, you are going to give quickness. Unfortunately, since there is no weapon swapping on this build with Berserk, your quickness uptime is going to be a bit iffy. You can either slot in longbow and use your F1s there, or if you want to camp sword/sword and give quickness, I would recommend looking at boon duration food and boon duration stats. For example, you could look at Ritualist's. You are going to see there is a stat called concentration. Concentration gives you boon duration, so you can increase the boon duration a little bit. Maybe you can more easily get away with playing it as quickness. You can also look at Firebrand runes as well.

Basically, if you are going to play this as quickness and you are only going to do the sword/sword thing, then look at something that gives you quickness duration or boon duration and slot that in. Otherwise, the quickness on this build can be a bit spotty. You certainly can make it work, because you can see that I get quickness with F1s, but it can be a little spotty. You can probably play it as is, but I would recommend slotting in just a tiny bit of boon duration if you decide to do it.

items.title: Paragon Low Intensity Power 31k/36k
items.explanation:
This is a beginner/low intensity warrior build for fractal/raid/strike content.

0:00 1 Button Rotation
2:27 1 Button Explanation
2:40 5 Buttons Rotation
4:52 5 Buttons Explanation
6:31 Overall Build Explanation
items.cleaned_transcript:
For the one-button breakdown, all you want to do is spam F1. Not much to add. I do need you to watch the build rundown later in the video, so please skip to it.

For the five buttons, all you want to do is spam your Signet of Stamina and Dolyak Signet. These signets have no cast time, so basically you can use them whenever. You can use them mid-animation. For example, if I use dagger 2, you can see that I will be able to use my Signet of Stamina and Dolyak Signet during the cast time, so it will not interrupt anything. You just use them off cooldown.

Your opener is To the Limit! to gain adrenaline, then use your F2 into F1 to start off with 10 motivation, and then just use your skill 4 and skill 5. Axe and mace are really close in terms of damage. I found that axe beats it just a little bit for Paragon, unlike Spellbreaker, so honestly you can run whatever you want. You can either run mace or axe. I decided to go with axe, but you may run mace as well. The damage difference is not that much.

The benefits of axe in this case are slightly higher damage, slightly lower burst damage, and way more sustain with Dual Wielding and axe 5. Mace offers CC and more burst damage with the 4-5-4 combo.

For the build explanation, before you continue, I need you to consider playing Spellbreaker and not Paragon. I want you to really heavily consider it because Paragon has motivation. I know that the number looks a little bigger, but I do not want you to look at that number. Please heavily consider playing Spellbreaker.

Here is why: motivation. The way this works has a few wrinkles. Depending on your active chant, you are going to get a different bonus from Strengthening Stanzas. If you look at it, Strengthening Stanzas gives you different stuff depending on which refrain is effectively active. If you screw up and accidentally press F3, Chant of Recuperation, you lose 15% damage. If you use anything else, you instantly lose that damage boost. That is problem one. You have to make sure that you stay on F2. If you accidentally press F3 or F4, then you have to make sure you go back to F2.

Problem two is motivation. The way it works is that you can get from zero to 10 stacks of motivation if you use your burst skills. You are going to get stacks of motivation, and they are going to passively tick down as you are in the fight if you are not using your burst skills, because basically you trade motivation to passively give stuff. If you look at Brisk Pacing, you are going to see that at seven to 10 motivation, you get a big damage buff. You get less damage the less motivation you have, so it is really important to maintain seven-plus stacks of motivation.

Again, Spellbreaker does not have this problem. Spellbreaker damage is a teeny tiny bit worse. I want you to heavily consider playing Spellbreaker, because if you screw up and, for whatever reason, cannot maintain seven-plus stacks of motivation, or you just forget, then you are going to have a bad time.

The easy tell if you do decide to play this is your character will have golden wings. You will see golden wings if the character has seven-plus stacks of motivation. You do not have to look down at your motivation bar. You can just visually look at your character and see that you have golden wings and you are doing what you are supposed to be doing. When you drop below seven stacks, the golden wings are gone. That is what the golden wings mean. That means you have seven-plus stacks of motivation and you are doing the right thing. But again, I heavily recommend you not play this.

If you decide to play it, here is the rundown. Skill 2 is a movement skill. You can get around and do stuff. Skill 3 is CC if you are playing axes. Again, if you did not catch what I said earlier, axe or mace are both viable. Axe seemed to do a teeny tiny bit more damage, but both have trade-offs, so feel free to play whatever you want. Axe or mace does not really matter. Skill 4 and skill 5 are just damage.

For the traits, I already briefly mentioned it, but Paragon has a whole bunch of problems with the DPS build that make it not really good for low intensity, even though the numbers look good. At least the power variant has that issue. The condi variant kind of passes by because it is simply strong enough that you do pay the motivation management fee, but again, consider Spellbreaker unless you are a Paragon stan.

This build crit caps using Unsuspecting Foe. Unsuspecting Foe basically gives you 25% extra crit chance against foes with defiance bars, so this is how you crit cap.

Signet Mastery works like this: every time you use a signet, you gain one minute of this 100 ferocity bonus. You can stack it up to five times. I did not show it in this video, but before the fight starts, you can be sitting there in the raid and just spam your signets to stack up that signet ferocity bonus. You can start at five stacks whenever the fight starts, and this buff does not go away when the encounter starts. You are going to have a free 500 ferocity when you start the fight. Pretty nice, pretty good.

Dual Wielding gives a lot of extra healing. This is part of the mace versus axe debate, where axe is going to offer you more sustain than mace with Dual Wielding.

I already talked about the issues with motivation, so now you know this exists, and it is completely up to you if you want to play it. But I will warn you that this exists.

For utilities, Paragon actually has some good stuff. There is a bit of extra barrier and healing from Chant of Recuperation. Chant of Freedom is the useful one. This is basically built into your kit, where you have stability and a stun break to give to your allies. But again, if you use this, you want to be careful. You pop Chant of Freedom and then go back to Chant of Action right away to not lose that damage buff.

There is a decent amount of useful utility. We Shall Return! is condition removal for you and your allies, so it is pretty good. There are more selfish healing skills that you can consider running, like Mending for a whole lot of selfish condi cleanse, and Defiant Stance. But if you decide to swap out To the Limit!, you can maybe consider swapping one of your signets to Signet of Fury, because you need something to give you that motivation. But anyway, probably just do not swap the healing skill.

If you are going to swap any of these utilities, here are some of the useful ones. Banner of Defense gives aegis. Banner of Tactics gives stability. Shake It Off! is a group stun break and condi cleanse, so it is really good.

All of these bottom skills here are CC skills. Bull's Charge and Stomp are all CC skills. If you need more CC skills, then go ahead and equip them. On Your Knees! is a decent amount of defiance break, so again, just more CC. I would probably recommend you run On Your Knees! over these skills, but you do get the Physical bonus from Peak Performance using one of these skills down here, so it is up to you. I think Never Surrender! is also aegis and a stun break. These are some of the useful skills that you can consider running.

Again, my final recommendation for you if you are looking to play this build is to play Spellbreaker. If you absolutely cannot play Spellbreaker, then I wish you the best of luck in managing your motivation. But if you do decide to do it, then make sure you stay at seven-plus motivation.

If you want to play this build as an alacrity build, all you have to do is change the last trait of the Paragon line from Brisk Pacing to Feverish Pulse. Obviously, you are going to do a lot less damage, but what this will do is make it so anytime you use a chant skill, and your chant skills are your F2, F3, and F4, the unique Paragon skills that you get, you will give about seven seconds of alacrity.

If you are looking to play this as an alacrity DPS, as an alacrity giver in raids, strikes, or more likely fractals, you want to slot in Feverish Pulse and cycle through your chants. The way this works is, for example, let us say I am starting combat. I am going to use To the Limit!, I am going to use my F2, and I am going to use my F1. You are going to see that I am giving some alacrity uptime.

You can use your chants to give alacrity. Using just one of your chants off cooldown is not going to be quite enough. You can see that my alacrity keeps dropping. What you can do is cycle through your chants with all of your extra adrenaline. Since all you do is camp your weapon set, as an example, I am going to do F4, F3, and F2. Now I have given about 15 seconds of alacrity. Now all I can do is continue cycling through Chant of Action, and if I am feeling it, I can do F3, F2 to give even more alacrity. That is all I really need to do to play this as an alacrity DPS build. Just use your extra adrenaline to cycle through your chants so that you may give alacrity.

items.title: 3 Button Berserker Power ~31k
items.rotation: Spam F1s + Blood Reckoning. Try not to cancel your auto attack chain. Spam Outrage off cooldown.
items.explanation: This is a beginner/low intensity warrior build for fractal/raid/strike content.

items.title: 6 Button Berserker Power ~34k
items.rotation: This is a priority based rotation. The priority in order is: F1 (Burst) - Blood Reckoning into F1 - GS 2 (Hundred Blades) - GS 4 (Bladetrail) - GS 5 (Rush). You make a decision on what to cast between auto attack chains. Do not cancel auto attack chains. Spam outrage off cool down for berserk uptime.
items.explanation:
This is a beginner warrior build for fractal/raid/strike content.

Looking back over it I made a few minor mistakes I think 39.5k+ is possible if you play it well.

items.title: 4 Button Paragon Alacrity Healer
items.rotation: Cast "To The Limit!" (heal), "For Great Justice!", F3, F1 off cooldown.
items.explanation:
This is a beginner/low intensity warrior build for fractal/raid/strike content.

You will (probably) not be able to solo heal a squad or the build may not work well on more threatening/higher damaging scenario. If you play this the overheal from the other healer will be assisting you some. Or if people are eating a lot of damage it will also not work well and you have to press your skills. In those scenarios Staff 2 Staff 3 Staff 5 are good burst healing skills to press. I focused low intensity on providing good boons + sustained healing. Unfortunately healers need to be versatile somewhat and adapt to the situation.
